attributes.stem:
A previously well 62-year-old woman has a CT scan performed as part of investigation of ongoing right loin pain which radiates to her groin. She is found to have a 9mm stone at her right pelvi-ureteric junction and in addition, there is a 5mm stone detected in the lower calyx in the right kidney. She has never had any previous urinary problems. Urine dipstick test shows a trace of protein, trace of blood and is negative for nitrites. Serum and urine biochemistry:

  • Serum creatinine 118 µmol/L (60 - 110)

  • Serum calcium (corrected) 2.55 mmol/L (2.10 - 2.60)

  • Serum urate 0.55 mmol/L (0.20 - 0.45)

  • Urinary calcium excretion 0.55 mol/mol Cr (0.04 - 0.45)

Arrangements are made for a ureteroscopic retrieval of the 9mm stone at the pelvi-ureteric junction.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management for the stone in the lower calyx?
options: Leave alone and review in one year
attributes.explanation:
The patient is diagnosed as a case of unilateral kidney stones in two locations, the pelvic-ureteric junction one measuring 9mm is causing symptoms for which a ureteroscopic procedure is chosen. The lower calyx right kidney stone is also removed during the primary procedures by using simultaneous ureteroscopy because according to research current thinking is that secondary, asymptomatic renal calculi should be treated at the same time as the primary stone is being treated. It should not be assumed that this stone in the lower calyx is asymptomatic and since a right ureteroscopy is being performed for the stone at the pelvic-ureteric junction, it makes sense to remove this additional stone.

Extracorporeal lithotripsy (ESWL) consists of using shockwaves to break stones, it is the most appropriate approach for a stone of this size which is not causing any obstructive or infectious problems. But since the patient is already undergoing ureteroscopy for a right kidney stone, simultaneous ureteroscopy would be preferred.

Leaving alone and reviewing in one year can be an appropriate management plan if a very small stone (<10 mm) is encountered during routine diagnostic procedures and is not causing any symptoms of obstruction. However in this case, because the stone is ipsilateral and can grow in the future and then the patient has to go another procedure; it makes sense to remove the stone when the patient is already undergoing an invasive procedure. If this stone was in the left side then it would be managed with leaving and reviewing in one year.

Medical expulsion therapy consists of pharmacological therapy to induce the explosion of the stones it is generally reserved for stones that are lower in the urinary tract and are less than <8mm in diameter.

Percutaneous nephrolithotomy consists of creating a tract into the renal collecting system using ultrasound or fluoroscopic guidance. It is indicated for the treatment of larger stones in the renal pelvis or calyces, when ESWL is contraindicated, and when the stones are composed of struvite because of the associated risk of infection.
learningPoints: Simultaneous ureteroscopy is employed for the treatment of secondary, asymptomatic renal calculi.
references: Sorensen MD, Harper JD, Borofsky MS, et al. Removal of Small, Asymptomatic Kidney Stones and Incidence of Relapse. N Engl J Med 2022; 387:506-513

Sabler IM, Katafigiotis I, Gofrit ON, Duvdevani M. Present indications and techniques of percutaneous nephrolithotomy: What the future holds? Asian J Urol. 2018 Oct;5(4):287-294. doi: 10.1016/j.ajur.2018.08.004. Epub 2018 Sep 6. PMID: 30364501; PMCID: PMC6197369.
Manzoor H, Saikali SW. Renal Extracorporeal Lithotripsy. [Updated 2022 Jul 25]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-.
